title: "Scarlet Falcon Puzzle v17.19"

  description: "At 20:35, the Scarlet Falcon—a prized statuette—vanished from a private gallery. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had keycard access around that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three are fully truthful. There is only one culprit overall. Each suspect provides three claims:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I was nowhere near the Falcon display after 20:25.'
      (2) 'If 2 is telling the truth, then 4 left at 20:30.'
      (3) 'Suspect 3 is definitely innocent—there's no way 3 stole it.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) 'Suspect 1 actually lingered until 20:40.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4 must be lying too.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty, and I had left by 20:20.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never set foot in that gallery after 20:15.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is guilty, or 1 is lying about the time—but not both.'
      (3) '4 is certainly not the culprit; I saw them in a different wing.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) 'Suspect 3 was indeed near the Falcon around 20:25—contradicting 3’s claim.'
      (2) 'If 1 is truthful, then 2 cannot be innocent.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty—someone else is the real thief (likely 2 or 3).'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Scarlet Falcon? Please return one digit only."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Logic Derivation:

  Let L(x) mean 'x lies in all their statements' and T(x) mean 'x is truthful in all statements,' G(x) mean 'x is guilty.'  
  Exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, the rest T(x).  

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3) are false:
    - (1) false => 1 might have been near display after 20:25 => possible guilt.
    - (2) false => if 2 is truthful => 4 left at 20:30 is not guaranteed => conflict.
    - (3) false => 3 is not definitely innocent => 3 could be culprit. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => check 4(1) says '3 was near Falcon at 20:25' => that conflicts with 3(1) 'never after 20:15' but 3 is truthful => contradiction arises. Fails.  

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3) are all false:
    - (1) false => '1 lingered until 20:40' is false => 1’s claim of 'nowhere after 20:25' might stand.
    - (2) false => if 3 is lying => 4 also lying is not correct => no forced scenario.
    - (3) false => 2 could be guilty or stayed past 20:20. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(3) '3 definitely innocent' => G(3)=false. 4(3) 'I not guilty => suspect 2 or 3' => if 4 is not guilty => we already have 3=innocent => G(2)=true. No contradictions.  

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3) are false:
    - (1) false => 3 did set foot after 20:15 => possible guilt.
    - (2) false => 'Either 2 guilty or 1 lying time not both' fails => maybe both or neither => complicated. 
    - (3) false => 4 might indeed be culprit. But let's see if 1,2,4 are truthful => 4(1) '3 near Falcon at 20:25' consistent with 3(1) false => G(3)? Possibly. Times might or might not produce contradiction. Checking carefully can fit G(3).  

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3) are false => '3 near Falcon 20:25' is false => 3 might be honest about 20:15, but 3 is presumably truthful => no direct conflict. However, 2(2) 'If 3 lying => 4 lying too' is true => we see timeline inconsistencies. Contradictions appear.  

  We find L(2) or L(3) each can yield consistent logic. Evaluate:

  - If L(2), from 1(3) '3 is definitely innocent' => G(3)=false, 4(3) 'not guilty => suspect 2 or 3' => 3 false => so G(2)=true.  
  - If L(3), 1,2,4 truthful => 4(1) '3 near 20:25' => G(3)=true.  

  We must see if both can be valid or if only one stands. Look at 2(3) false => 'I left by 20:20' is also false => so 2 could have been near the scene. Also 1(2) 'If 2 truthful => 4 left 20:30' doesn't apply if 2 is lying. Typically, puzzle solutions force one unique. Checking times more thoroughly suggests L(2) scenario is simpler with no contradiction => G(2). The L(3) scenario might cause a mismatch with 1(3) if 3 is guilty but also '3 definitely innocent'? Contradiction.  

  Hence final consistent scenario is L(2) => G(2).  

  final: '2'"
